title: Reading and Use of English – Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Rise of the Minimalist Lifestyle
content.subtitle: How the 'less is more' philosophy is challenging the foundations of consumerism.
content.text: In a world that equates success with the accumulation of possessions, the growing popularity of minimalism feels like a radical rebellion. Minimalists advocate for living with only the things that serve a purpose or bring joy, intentionally discarding the excess. [31] The writer suggests that this movement is not just about home organization, but a psychological liberation from the 'clutter' of the modern mind. [/31] It is an attempt to regain control over our attention, which is often hijacked by the maintenance and desire for material goods. || This shift is partly a reaction to 'Decision Fatigue.' In a typical day, we are faced with thousands of choices—from which brand of toothpaste to buy to which of a dozen streaming services to watch. [32] Minimalists argue that by reducing the number of physical items we own, we reduce the 'cognitive load' required to manage our lives, freeing up mental energy for more meaningful pursuits. [/32] When our environment is simplified, our internal world follows suit. We move from a state of 'reacting' to our surroundings to 'acting' with intention. || [33] The author's tone when discussing the environmental impact of consumerism is one of urgent concern. [/33] Our 'throwaway culture' has led to a global crisis of waste and resource depletion. Minimalism offers a practical alternative: by buying fewer, higher-quality items that last longer, we can significantly reduce our ecological footprint. It is a move away from 'quantity' and toward 'utility.' However, this requires a fundamental shift in how we view value. We must learn to value the *experience* of an item rather than the status of owning it. || Another dimension of minimalism is its impact on personal finance. [34] By opting out of the 'hedonic treadmill'—the constant cycle of buying new things to maintain a baseline level of happiness—individuals find they need significantly less money to live well. [/34] This financial freedom often allows people to pursue careers they find more fulfilling, even if they pay less, or to work fewer hours and spend more time with family. Minimalism, in this sense, is a tool for life design. It is not about deprivation; it is about making space for what truly matters. || [35] Some critics, however, argue that minimalism is a 'privilege of the wealthy.' [/35] They suggest that only those who already have a financial safety net can afford to get rid of their belongings, knowing they can easily replace them if needed. For those living in poverty, having 'extra' things is a form of insurance against an uncertain future. This highlight a valid tension within the movement: for minimalism to be a universal philosophy, it must address the different needs and anxieties of various social classes. || Ultimately, the minimalist journey is a deeply personal one. [36] The writer concludes that the goal is not to live in an empty room, but to live a life that is not defined by its contents. [/36] It is an ongoing practice of questioning our motivations and being mindful of what we allow into our space and our minds. In an age of infinite noise and clutter, the most valuable thing we can possess is the clarity of our own purpose. The 'less' is not the point; the 'more' that comes with it—more time, more energy, more peace—is where the real value lies.

questions.question: In the first paragraph, the author suggests that minimalism...
options: A) is primarily a way to make a home look more attractive.
B) is a rebellion against the traditional idea of success.
C) is a trend that will only last for a few more years.
D) is impossible to achieve in a modern city.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [31] and the mention of a 'radical rebellion' against the accumulation of possessions. A is the 'home organization' aspect that the author says is only part of the story. C and D are not mentioned.

questions.question: According to the second paragraph, reducing the items we own can help us...
options: A) make faster decisions about which products to buy.
B) increase the amount of physical space we have for new hobbies.
C) decrease the mental energy needed to manage our daily lives.
D) spend more time watching different streaming services.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [32] and the mention of reducing the 'cognitive load.' A is the problem (Decision Fatigue) that minimalism seeks to avoid. B and D are not mentioned.

questions.question: What does the author believe is necessary to reduce our 'ecological footprint'?
options: A) Buying as many cheap items as possible to save money.
B) A change in the way we perceive the value of an object.
C) Getting rid of all material goods and living in nature.
D) Finding more efficient ways to recycle our existing waste.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [33] and the need for a 'fundamental shift in how we view value.' A is the 'throwaway culture' the author criticizes. C is too extreme. D is a solution but not the one the author emphasizes in the context of minimalism.

questions.question: The 'hedonic treadmill' mentioned in the fourth paragraph refers to...
options: A) a specific type of exercise equipment used by minimalists.
B) the process of working harder to buy a larger house.
C) the cycle of buying things to keep up a certain level of happiness.
D) a financial strategy for investing in high-quality products.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [34] and the description of the 'constant cycle of buying new things to maintain a baseline level of happiness.' A is a literal misinterpretation. B and D are not mentioned.

questions.question: What is the main criticism of minimalism mentioned in the fifth paragraph?
options: A) That it is too difficult for most people to understand.
B) That it can only be practiced by people who are already financially secure.
C) That it causes people to become less interested in social issues.
D) That it leads to a decrease in the quality of the things we own.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [35] and the idea of it being a 'privilege of the wealthy.' A, C, and D are not mentioned as criticisms in the text.

questions.question: Which of the following best summarizes the writer's conclusion?
options: A) The ultimate goal of minimalism is to own absolutely nothing.
B) Minimalists should focus on living in completely empty rooms.
C) Minimalism is about defining your life by its purpose rather than its objects.
D) Clarity is only possible for people who live a quiet, rural life.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [36] and the idea that life should not be 'defined by its contents.' A and B are the 'empty room' image the author says is *not* the goal. D is not suggested.
